metric_value: Jointly announced shared vision of tomorrow's enterprise data center based on standardized technologies dominated by clusters of small servers running Linux

metric_value: Dell and Oracle predict demise of big iron (mainframes/large Unix) in favor of commodity Linux clusters
Oracle9i RAC enables clustered database on commodity Linux hardware starting at $18,000
notes: Dell/Oracle clusters with Oracle9i RAC; Red Hat Linux

metric_value: Standardization, scale-out, on-demand provisioning, grid computing identified as architectural principles
notes: Keywords: clusters, Linux, standardization, scale-out, blades, provisioning, on-demand, grid

metric_value: Blade servers identified as key form factor for commodity cluster architecture
notes: Blades referenced alongside clusters in the data center vision

metric_value: IBM Z mainframes still active in ~6000 shops worldwide as of 2025; mainframe not demised; prediction was overstated
notes: Mainframes proved resilient for transaction-intensive workloads; prediction was partially wrong

metric_name: Commodity Linux Cluster Dominance - Actual Outcome
metric_value: Commodity Linux clusters became dominant substrate for cloud computing (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud); prediction correct for new workloads
notes: Dell/Oracle were correct about cloud-new workloads; wrong about mainframe displacement

metric_value: Grid computing branded concept was superseded by cloud computing circa 2006-2010; utility computing vision was correct, brand was not
notes: Grid -> cloud transition; Oracle's 15,000+ grid users became Oracle Cloud
Per The Register coverage of Dell/Oracle announcement April 2003

metric_value: Oracle identified Linux as fastest growing OS for any deployment; aligned with Dell on commodity server vision

metric_value: Systems management identified as key challenge/requirement for large Linux cluster deployments
